svg.text: How plugins are grouped, assigned to waves, and executed in parallel — showing CPU ‖ GPU concurrency
User declares plugins in YAML order.
all gpu → merged; each worker gets one GPU device
plan_plugin_groups() assigns each group to a wave
subject to: (a) all dependencies in earlier waves,
(b) at most one pool per wave per slot.
gpu-only wave: waits for Group 1 (interface data needed)
Each wave is a list of groups that can run in parallel.
run_plugins() drives the wave loop. Each wave launches
all its groups in parallel via a ThreadPoolExecutor
(one thread per pool: cpu thread + gpu thread).
Wave 0 — two cpu groups run concurrently
Wave 0: both CPU groups run simultaneously (no GPU work needed yet)
GPU thread (one process per device slot)
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES set per worker via initializer
gpu-only wave — GPU workers saturated; CPU is idle this wave
Each group's workers come from this context manager — prefers ProcessPool,
falls back to ThreadPool on PermissionError (containers, macOS sandbox)
Two specs merge into one group only when:
• the later spec doesn't require the earlier (no intra-group deps)
Topological order from the requires DAG is preserved.
Groups are executed as an atomic block with shared workers.
Group is placed in the lowest wave where:
• all dependency groups are in earlier waves
• its worker_pool slot is not already occupied in that wave
Result: CPU-only and GPU-only groups share a wave when independent.
A dependent group is pushed to the next wave automatically.
Each slot gets its own ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES set per worker via initializer.
gpu_devices_per_worker=1 enforced; each worker sees exactly one GPU.
to launch all groups in a wave simultaneously.
Each thread calls _execute_plugin_group() for one group.
Wave N+1 only starts after all futures in Wave N resolve.
This is the ~minimum_atomworks parallelism narrative in one picture.
scheduling logic: core/_schedule.py · execution engine: core/_execute.py · pool fallback: runtime/_pool.py
